title: Python Basics
description: Introduction to Python syntax, variables, and control flow.

tutorial.title: What is a Variable?
tutorial.content:
A variable is like a labeled box where you can store data.
Unlike some other languages, Python doesn't require you to declare the type
of variable ahead of time. You just assign a value to a name, and Python
figures it out automatically — this is called 'dynamic typing'.

tutorial.title: Creating Variables
tutorial.content:
count = 10          # Integer
pi = 3.14           # Float
name = 'AI'         # String
is_active = True    # Boolean

tutorial.title: The 4 Basic Types
tutorial.content:
int   → Whole numbers        → 0, 42, -7
float → Decimal numbers      → 3.14, -0.5
str   → Text (in quotes)     → 'hello', "world"
bool  → True or False only   → True, False

Python chooses the type based on the value you assign.

tutorial.title: Pro Tip: Naming Conventions
tutorial.content:
Use descriptive names! 'x' is bad, 'user_age' is good.
Python uses snake_case (words_separated_by_underscores).
Variable names cannot start with a number or contain spaces.

  ✅  user_name = 'Alice'
  ❌  1st_name  = 'Bob'      (starts with number)
  ❌  my name   = 'Carol'    (contains space)

tutorial.title: Common Mistake: = vs ==
tutorial.content:
These look similar but mean very different things:

  x = 5    →  Assignment  (store 5 in x)
  x == 5   →  Comparison  (is x equal to 5? Returns True/False)

Mixing them up is one of the most common beginner mistakes!

tutorial.title: Try It: Type Checking
tutorial.content:
my_var = 42
print(type(my_var))  # <class 'int'>

my_var = 'Hello'
print(type(my_var))  # <class 'str'>

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: Which of the following is a valid Python variable name?

tutorial.title: Making Decisions
tutorial.content:
Programs need to make choices. We use 'if' statements to run code only
when a certain condition is true.

Think of it like a fork in the road — your program takes a different
path depending on some condition.

tutorial.title: If-Elif-Else Example
Comparison Operators
tutorial.content:
==   Equal to
!=   Not equal to
<    Less than
>    Greater than
<=   Less than or equal
>=   Greater than or equal

These always return True or False.

tutorial.title: Pro Tip: Combining Conditions
tutorial.content:
Use 'and' / 'or' to combine conditions:
  if age >= 18 and has_license:
      print('Can drive')

Use 'not' to negate:
  if not is_banned:
      print('Welcome!')

Short-circuit evaluation: Python stops early if the result is clear.

tutorial.title: Common Mistake: Indentation
tutorial.content:
Python uses INDENTATION (spaces) to define code blocks.
Forgetting to indent is a very common error!

  ✅  if x > 0:
         print('positive')   # 4 spaces

  ❌  if x > 0:
      print('positive')   # Wrong! No indent after if

tutorial.title: Try It: Grade Calculator
Repeating Actions
tutorial.content:
Loops allow you to repeat a block of code multiple times.
The 'for' loop is great for iterating over a sequence of items.

Instead of writing:
  print(0)
  print(1)
  print(2)

You write:
  for i in range(3):
      print(i)

tutorial.title: For Loop Example
tutorial.content:
for i in range(5):
    print(i)

tutorial.title: The range() Function
tutorial.content:
range() generates a sequence of numbers.

  range(5)       →  0, 1, 2, 3, 4
  range(2, 6)    →  2, 3, 4, 5
  range(0, 10, 2) →  0, 2, 4, 6, 8  (step of 2)

Note: The end value is EXCLUDED.

tutorial.title: Iterating Over Lists
tutorial.content:
You can also loop over any list directly:

  fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
  for fruit in fruits:
      print(fruit)

This is much more readable than using indexes.

tutorial.title: Pro Tip: enumerate()
tutorial.content:
# Get index AND value at the same time!
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
for idx, fruit in enumerate(fruits):
    print(f'{idx}: {fruit}')

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: How many times does `for i in range(3, 7):` execute?

tutorial.title: The While Loop
tutorial.content:
A 'while' loop repeats as long as a condition remains true.
Use it when you don't know how many times you need to loop.

Be careful: ensure the condition eventually becomes false,
or you'll get an infinite loop!

tutorial.title: While Loop Example
tutorial.content:
count = 3
while count > 0:
    print(count)
    count -= 1
print('Done!')

tutorial.title: break and continue
For vs While — When to Use Which?
tutorial.content:
Use FOR when:
  ✅ You know the number of iterations
  ✅ Iterating over a list, string, or range

Use WHILE when:
  ✅ You don't know how many times to repeat
  ✅ Waiting for user input or a condition to change

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: What keyword exits a loop immediately?

tutorial.title: Reusable Code
tutorial.content:
Functions let you group code into reusable blocks.
You give them a name, pass in data (arguments), and can get back a result.

Without functions, you'd repeat the same code everywhere —
functions follow the DRY principle: Don't Repeat Yourself!

tutorial.title: Defining and Calling Functions
tutorial.content:
def greet(name):
    return f'Hello, {name}!'

print(greet('Alice'))
print(greet('Bob'))

tutorial.title: Parameters vs Arguments
tutorial.content:
Parameters are variables listed in the function definition.
Arguments are the actual values passed when calling the function.

  def add(a, b):       # a and b are PARAMETERS
      return a + b

  add(3, 4)            # 3 and 4 are ARGUMENTS

tutorial.title: Default Arguments
tutorial.content:
You can give parameters a default value.
That way, calling the function without that argument still works.

  def power(base, exponent=2):
      return base ** exponent

  power(3)     →  9  (uses default exponent=2)
  power(3, 3)  →  27 (overrides default)

tutorial.title: Try It: Function with Default
tutorial.content:
def power(base, exponent=2):
    return base ** exponent

print(power(4))     # 16  (squared)
print(power(2, 10)) # 1024 (2^10)

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: Which keyword is used to send a value back from a function?

tutorial.title: Interacting with Users
tutorial.content:
You can make your programs interactive by asking the user for input
using the input() function.

Important: input() ALWAYS returns a string, even if the user types a number.
You must convert it if you need a number.

tutorial.title: Input Example
tutorial.content:
name = input('What is your name? ')
print(f'Hello, {name}!')

tutorial.title: Converting Input Types
tutorial.content:
Since input() always returns a string, you must cast it:

  age_str = input('Your age: ')
  age = int(age_str)      # Convert to integer
  year = float(input())   # Can chain directly

Always use try/except when converting user input —
the user might type something unexpected!

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: What type does input() always return?

tutorial.title: Python Operators
tutorial.content:
Operators are special symbols in Python that carry out arithmetic
or logical computation. They are the building blocks of every program.

tutorial.title: Arithmetic Operators
tutorial.content:
x = 10
y = 3
print(x + y)   # Addition:       13
print(x - y)   # Subtraction:    7
print(x * y)   # Multiplication: 30
print(x / y)   # Division:       3.333...
print(x // y)  # Floor Division: 3
print(x % y)   # Modulo:         1
print(x ** y)  # Exponentiation: 1000

tutorial.title: Floor Division and Modulo
tutorial.content:
These two are very useful in programming:

  //  Floor Division → result rounded DOWN to nearest int
      17 // 5  =  3  (not 3.4)

  %   Modulo → remainder after division
      17 % 5   =  2  (17 = 5*3 + 2)

Practical use: x % 2 == 0 tells you if x is even!

tutorial.title: Logical Operators
tutorial.content:
t = True
f = False
print(t and f)   # False  (both must be True)
print(t or f)    # True   (at least one must be True)
print(not t)     # False  (flips the boolean)

tutorial.title: Type Conversion
tutorial.content:
Sometimes you need to convert a variable from one data type to another.
This is called Type Casting.

  int('42')   → 42
  float('3.14') → 3.14
  str(100)    → '100'
  bool(0)     → False

tutorial.title: Explicit Type Casting
tutorial.content:
num_str = '123'
num_int = int(num_str)
print(num_int + 7)   # 130

float_val = float('3.14')
print(float_val)     # 3.14

str_val = str(42)
print('Answer: ' + str_val)  # 'Answer: 42'

tutorial.title: Truthy and Falsy Values
tutorial.content:
Every value in Python can be treated as True or False:

  Falsy values: 0, 0.0, '', [], {}, None
  Truthy values: everything else

This is useful in conditions:
  if username:         # True if username is not empty
      print('Hello!')

tutorial.title: Truthiness Demo
tutorial.content:
print(bool('hello'))  # True
print(bool(''))       # False
print(bool(0))        # False
print(bool(42))       # True
print(bool([]))       # False — empty list
